data.title: Is Markel Group (MKL) Pricing Reflect Its Mixed Returns And Intrinsic Value Estimates
data.description:

Approach 1: Markel Group Excess Returns Analysis

The Excess Returns model looks at how much profit a company is expected to earn over and above the return that shareholders could reasonably ask for on their capital. It compares the return on equity to the cost of equity, then capitalises those “excess” profits on top of the book value per share.

For Markel Group, book value is $1,477.23 per share, while stable book value is estimated at $1,722.47 per share, based on weighted future book value estimates from 3 analysts. Stable EPS is $149.86 per share, sourced from weighted future return on equity estimates from 4 analysts, and the average return on equity is 8.70%.

The cost of equity is $120.19 per share, so the model estimates an excess return of $29.66 per share. When these excess returns are projected and added to the stable book value, the intrinsic value from this Excess Returns model is $2,553.78 per share.

Against a current share price of about $1,929.91, this points to an implied 24.4% discount, which suggests Markel Group may be undervalued on this measure.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Approach 2: Markel Group Price vs Earnings

For a profitable company like Markel Group, the P/E ratio is a useful way to think about what you are paying for each dollar of current earnings. Investors usually accept a higher P/E when they expect stronger earnings growth or see lower risk, and a lower P/E when growth expectations are more modest or the perceived risk is higher.

Markel Group currently trades on a P/E of 11.31x. That sits close to both the Insurance industry average P/E of 11.62x and the peer group average of 12.28x, so the market is pricing Markel Group in a similar range to its sector and peers. Simply Wall St also calculates a proprietary “Fair Ratio” of 11.00x, which is the P/E it would expect for Markel Group given its earnings profile, industry, profit margins, market cap and risk factors.

This Fair Ratio can be more informative than a simple comparison with peers or industry averages because it tries to tailor the expected multiple to the company’s own characteristics rather than broad groupings. data.title: First American Financial (FAF) Margin Rebound Tests Bullish Profitability Narratives Ahead Of Q1 2026
data.description:
First American Financial (FAF) has followed up its earnings rebound with trailing 12‑month revenue of about US$7.5 billion and basic EPS of US$6.02, alongside year‑over‑year reported earnings growth of very roughly 4x and a net profit margin of 8.3% versus 2.1% a year earlier. The company has seen quarterly revenue move from US$1,406.1 million in Q3 2024 to US$1,685.1 million in Q4 2024 and then to US$2,049.7 million in Q4 2025, with basic EPS shifting from a loss of US$1.00 in Q3 2024 to US$0.70 in Q4 2024 and then US$2.06 in Q4 2025. This sets up the Q1 2026 release against a backdrop of sharply improved profitability. For investors, the current numbers point to a business where margins have moved from being a concern to becoming a key focus of the story.

See our full analysis for First American Financial.

With the latest earnings context in place, the next step is to see how these results line up with the most widely held narratives about First American Financial and where the data pushes back on those stories.

See what the community is saying about First American Financial

Four straight quarters above US$1.5b revenue

  • Over the last four reported quarters, revenue ranged from US$1,582.3 million in Q1 2025 up to US$2,049.7 million in Q4 2025, keeping the run rate above US$1.5b throughout the year.
  • Analysts' consensus view that diverse revenue streams can smooth out real estate cycles is partly backed by this steady revenue band, yet:
    • Trailing twelve month revenue sits at US$7.5b compared with US$5,872.3 million a year earlier, while the narrative still flags sensitivity to home affordability and mortgage rates.
    • Commercial strength is highlighted as a support for earnings stability, but the same consensus also points out that unusually high fee deals could fade, which is not directly visible in the top line figures alone.

EPS climbs from loss to US$6.02 TTM

  • Basic EPS moved from a loss of US$1.00 in Q3 2024 to US$2.06 in Q4 2025, and on a trailing twelve month basis reached US$6.02 alongside net income of US$621.8 million.
  • What stands out for the bullish narrative is that this sharp earnings swing, with reported earnings up about 4x year over year and net margin at 8.3%, aligns with claims about tech investment and automation supporting higher margins, yet:
    • The same consensus expects profit margins to ease slightly to 8.2% over time, so current levels leave limited room for further expansion if that view proves accurate.
    • Home Warranty and commercial segments are described as important margin supports, but favorable loss ratios and unusually strong commercial activity are flagged as temporary, which contrasts with the very strong recent earnings profile.
On this kind of earnings swing, many bulls argue the margin story is just getting started, while others worry it rests on conditions that may not last, and that tension is front and center in the full bull case 🐂 First American Financial Bull Case

  • Critics highlight this valuation gap as a key bearish point, arguing that the price already reflects a lot of good news, and the data here gives them material support because:
    • The stock sits below the consensus analyst target of US$82.00, yet the large spread between the current price and the DCF fair value suggests very different views on long term cash generation.
    • The 3.2% dividend yield and the 8.3% net margin can support income and profitability narratives, but do not on their own close the gap between the market price and the DCF fair value figure.

data.title: Is Tencent Music Entertainment Group (NYSE:TME) Pricing Reflect Recent Slide And DCF Upside Potential

Approach 1: Tencent Music Entertainment Group Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model estimates what a business could be worth by projecting its future cash flows and then discounting those back to today’s value. It is essentially asking what Tencent Music Entertainment Group’s future cash in CN¥ is worth in today’s money.

Simply Wall St uses a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity model here. Tencent Music’s latest twelve month free cash flow is CN¥8,980.76m. Analyst and extrapolated projections suggest free cash flow of CN¥10,717.57m in 2026 and CN¥12,682m by 2030, with additional years filled in by the model rather than by direct analyst forecasts.

When all those projected cash flows are discounted back, the model arrives at an estimated intrinsic value of US$14.75 per share, compared with the recent share price of US$9.25. That implies the shares trade at about a 37.3% discount to this DCF estimate, which points to a meaningful gap between price and modelled value.

Higher growth expectations or lower perceived risk tend to justify a higher P/E, while slower growth or higher risk usually line up with a lower, more cautious multiple.

Tencent Music Entertainment Group currently trades on a P/E of 8.99x. That sits well below the Entertainment industry average P/E of 33.24x and the peer group average of 75.92x, which shows a wide gap between Tencent Music and many other earnings generating media and entertainment names.

data.title: FFO Growth At Essential Properties Realty Trust (EPRT) Tests Long Lease Stability Narrative
data.description:
Essential Properties Realty Trust (EPRT) has kicked off Q1 2026 with total revenue of US$158.8 million, basic EPS of US$0.28 and net income of US$59.8 million, with management and investors also watching FFO of US$114.6 million and FFO per share of US$0.54 as key REIT metrics. Over the past year, the company has seen revenue move from US$129.4 million and basic EPS of US$0.30 in Q1 2025 to US$158.8 million and US$0.28 in the latest quarter, while trailing twelve month revenue reached US$590.7 million and EPS came in at US$1.27. With earnings over the last year growing 20.9% and net profit margin at 43.3%, the release provides a clear view of a business where profitability remains central to the story.

FFO and Revenue Growth Outpace EPS Trend

  • FFO rose from US$92.1 million in Q1 2025 to US$114.6 million in Q1 2026, while total revenue moved from US$129.4 million to US$158.8 million over the same periods, even as basic EPS for the latest quarter sits at US$0.28.
  • Consensus narrative points to stable, inflation-linked cash flows from long leases and service-based tenants, and the numbers back that up in some ways but not others.
    • On the supportive side, trailing 12 month revenue of US$590.7 million and earnings growth of 20.9% suggest the portfolio is still converting rent into profits at scale, in line with the focus on long leases and near full occupancy.
    • On the other hand, the step down in Q1 2026 basic EPS from US$0.30 in Q1 2025 comes alongside a net margin of 43.3% that is slightly below last year’s 44.5%, so profit per share is not moving up as clearly as the FFO and revenue lines.

Consensus viewers who want to see how this earnings mix lines up with longer term storylines can dig deeper into the community angle here 📊 Read the what the Community is saying about Essential Properties Realty Trust.

Margins Stay High While Growth Moderates

  • Over the last 12 months, earnings grew 20.9% while the net profit margin sits at 43.3%, compared with 44.5% in the prior year. This 20.9% earnings growth is below the 5 year average of 24.8% per year in the dataset.
  • Consensus narrative talks about stable, predictable margin growth from inflation protected leases, and the current figures partly echo that story but also show some pressure.
    • High margins in the low 40% range fit with long leases and regular rent bumps that are expected to support steady profitability according to the narrative.
    • However, the small slip from 44.5% to 43.3%, together with forecasts that margins move lower over time, challenges the idea that profitability simply trends higher without any drag from rising expenses or sector specific risks.

Debt is flagged as not well covered by operating cash flow.

    • Supportive of a constructive view, earnings growth of 20.9% and a P/E that is lower than the 29.9x peer average combine with the gap to the US$95.49 DCF fair value and US$37.20 analyst target to frame the current US$31.50 price as well below some valuation models.
    • Balancing that, the warning that debt is not well covered by operating cash flow and a P/E above the broader Global REITs industry average of 15.4x both point to financial risk and a premium pricing versus many REITs, which sits awkwardly with claims of a straightforwardly strong balance sheet.
